nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving streams, temporary pools, swamps, freshwater marshes, ponds, ditches and aquaculture ponds throughout subtropical and tropical lowland West Africa. In the dry season it has been reported to shelter in shallow streams under tree roots, stones or riverbank holes.

decorPreferences: soft sand or fine smooth gravel substrate
driftwood and smooth rockwork for shelter

diet.notes: Generally accepts earthworms, bloodworms, blackworms, brine shrimp, raw squid, and sinking pellets formulated for clawed frogs or carnivorous fish. Adults have been reported to take small live or frozen prey readily. Feed sparingly two to three times per week to prevent obesity; juveniles need more frequent small meals. Tadpoles are filter-feeders consuming zooplankton in the wild.

goodWith: Larger peaceful fish too big to be swallowed
avoidWith: African Clawed Frogs (Xenopus laevis) due to outcompetition and chytrid cross-transmission risk

summary: A small fully-aquatic West African pipid frog (~4-6 cm), tropical congener of the African Clawed Frog. Predatory toward small tankmates, hardy in stable warm water, and a notable escape artist requiring a tight lid.
careNotes:
Xenopus tropicalis is the smaller, warmer-water relative of the better-known African Clawed Frog (Xenopus laevis). It is widely used in developmental biology as the diploid model clawed frog, but is less common in the home-aquarium trade than X. laevis. The species is sometimes listed under its older genus name Silurana tropicalis. It can be distinguished from X. laevis by its smaller adult size and by having four claws on each hind foot rather than three.

Husbandry largely parallels X. laevis but at a smaller scale and warmer temperature: sources generally advise water in the 24-28 C range (cooler temperatures tolerated by X. laevis are not appropriate here). The species is hardy in terms of pH and hardness — published care guides describe it as tolerating a wide spectrum — but stable parameters around pH 6.5-7.8 are typically recommended.

Tank-size guidance varies: hobby sources cite 10-20 US gallons as a minimum for one or a small group, while research-colony SOPs use larger setups for groups. A 75 L (20-gallon long) footprint is used here as a cautious lower bound for a small group; singletons can be kept in less. Provide a tightly fitting cover — these frogs have been reported to climb and jump out of open tanks.

They are voracious predators relative to their size and have been known to eat any fish or invertebrate small enough to fit in their mouths. Best kept species-only or with significantly larger, robust tankmates that are not fin-nippers.

Like all Xenopus, this species is a known carrier and reservoir of chytrid fungus (Batrachochytrium dendrobatidis) and is implicated in the global spread of amphibian chytridiomycosis. Quarantine new specimens, do not release captive animals into the wild, and avoid mixing with other amphibians without veterinary screening.

Skin is permeable and sensitive to chlorine, chloramine, copper and many fish medications — dechlorinate all water, avoid copper-based ich treatments, and research medication safety before dosing.

Cross-source notes: Wikipedia lists adult SVL 28-55 mm; one hobby source reports specimens reaching ~10 cm (likely conflated with X. laevis); 4-6 cm is used here as the well-supported adult size. Lifespan figures range widely from 5 to 20 years across sources; 5-15 years is used as a moderate-confidence range.
breedingNotes: Breeding has been achieved in captivity and is routine in research settings. Spawning is generally triggered by simulating a seasonal drought followed by a cool water change, often combined with hCG injection in research contexts. Hobbyist breeding is reported as moderate difficulty and requires conditioned adult pairs, dim lighting and floating plants or surfaces for egg deposition. Females produce large clutches of single eggs that attach to plants or float freely. Generation time from egg to sexually mature adult is short (under 5 months), which is one reason the species is favored as a research model. Tadpoles are obligate filter feeders.

TFH Magazine's dedicated Silurana Clawed Frog article is a detailed hobby-aquarist care guide for this exact species.

amphibian.breedingNotes: Hormone-induced spawning is routine in research labs; hobbyist spawning is moderately difficult and typically requires simulating seasonal drought followed by a cool water change with dim lighting and floating substrates for egg attachment. Generation time from egg to maturity is under 5 months.
